segments.text: I've done a number of videos about indicators.
And one thing that I notice is that in the comments, when you talk about indicators,
you get two camps of people.
Right, I know there are people in the same, but the people that tend to comment are either
saying, thank you for providing the answer to all of my problems.
Now I can make millions of dollars. This is what I've been looking for.
Or it's indicators or the particular indicator you're talking about has no edge.
It's lagging. Like I only use charts. I only use chart patterns. I pride myself in that.
Your indicators are trash. And for some reason, and I don't know why, it awakens such a polarizing
stance. Right? Sure does. It's insane. And so, who is right?
I mean, out of both parties, first off, yeah, there are armies on both sides.
And I would argue they're both not right. The ones that say this indicator is the Lord of
Savior. I would argue that just one indicator alone will not help you trade every single setup
and a full, diverse playbook. And I would argue on the other end that if you're thinking an indicator
is lagging, we'll get into what even is an indicator because that's a whole different story.
But if you think I'm never going to use an indicator, it's lagging. You're missing out on a
ton of information that the market gives you every second. Right? I mean, indicators are not
strategies. Right? They're just tools. They're data points. They're ways for us to describe
variables of these strategies. And everything's lagging. Everything's historical data.
Like even your awesome work on fundamental analysis, Tim, is stuff that everybody knows or can
know. Right? Because it's out. It's out there. Right? Chart chart data is historical. Like I would
argue that candlestick charts is an indicator. High-low open close historical data of high-low
open close. We didn't always have that. Can you imagine trading back in 1929, you're just
you live or more. You're just that you're reading the ticker tape as it's coming out of the machine,
looking at the quotes. And then someone comes in with a computer and shows you candlestick charts
of the historical price data and explains this is the high-low open close of all of these time periods.
Like check out this indicator I created. You'd be like, this is wild. I'm like, it's only Jesse
Livermore didn't use MACD. Yeah, exactly. So, you know, I think in a way like I like to think of it as
broadening the idea of indicators. Right? The fact that like kind of any data that we look at in a
way is an indicator and it's not just, you know, your classic RSI and MACD. I mean,
Paul Tudor Jones, like what did he do? What was he doing? Yeah, I mean, that's kind of fun.
Let's think about it. Like, what was he doing 70s in 80s if he saw an overextended short like silver?
Like, it could have been in chord futures. But, I mean, he didn't use indicators like we're talking about
RSI. So there's a great YouTube movie. I think it's probably still on YouTube. I forget what it's
called, but you can probably just I've watched it. Yeah, you just type in Paul Tudor Jones movie or
something like that. And it follows him around during the 1987 crash. It's it's gold. And I mean,
you get all the vintage 80s vibes in there, but you get to see him on the phone. You get to see him
working with his assistant. And this is right in the thick of it. I think this was right before the
crash and they're talking about I think the market's still running up if I remember correctly. But
he gets with his assistant and his assistant has printed out all of these analogs to 1987. So I
think they had the big one was 1929. And they were taking the charts and they were super imposing them.
And comparing the run in 1929 to the run in 1987 and saying the same stuff that we've been talking
about, which is I think we're close. Because they're they're looking at the slope of the chart and
trying to figure out like, does this is this a check in favor? Does this qualify for something that
is going parabolic that is going to blow off? And they were they were I think they were long at the
time, but they were they were looking for the end. So he was looking at his playbook. Maybe it was
the Friday right before that Monday. And he was like, this is pretty similar in terms of an extension.
All right. I I agree. But it's in the way that's just indicators back then too. It was the candle
stick data. Yeah. So so indicators are just math. It's just statistics. You know, they're not
traders. They're not strategies. So we just think of them in a black and white fashion. If we want
the data, we get the data. That's that's all it is. And so it's really important to understand in my
opinion, what your indicators are measuring and and the nuances of them and when they work and when
they don't. So can you think of instances Tim where indicators are dangerous like using a certain
indicator might be completely the wrong thing to do? Yeah. Yeah. I have a couple. I would say one of
them would be a strategy that's just based on shorting RSI of 70 or buying RSI when it drops the 30.
Another example I would even say is with the moving averages where we only look at moving averages
certain moments when stocks are doing certain things and other times they're completely useless.
Like if a stock's been in a range for a year or let's say even a month range, it's been a month
range. Looking at the moving averages in trying to play like any moving average cross is just
going to lead to you getting chopped up and taking a million paper cuts. Okay. So so there are
there are certain signals that you don't like for instance the RSI on those default settings
which we can which we're going to get into or certain indicators have environments where they work
and where they don't. I totally agree with you about the the moving average thing because that's
it that's a trend if we're not in a trend the moving average is going to be meaningless. How do
you like to use indicators because you don't always have to just use it for an entry signal?
Like what are some of the ways that you like to use them?
Honestly I yeah I really don't use them for entry signals signals at all.
Indicators for me really just play into the context and we look at not just a single indicator
like we mentioned but we have ones that we're always looking at on our charts and I use them to
just give context for the playbook trades. So they're not trades alone like we mentioned
but the ones I like to use are a side the moving averages looking at the Keltner's and all that's
doing is telling me more about the stock and where it's at where it's positioned compared to the
market and it's the context. But I would never I would never honestly trade just off of it alone
and even our models like man we we don't have in trade off one like we have multiple entry criteria.
Right and I think another great way to use them is for filtering because you know you can
like someone might say oh I can just see it in the chart and that's great I think that's true
like you know especially if you can do that if you have those skills which I think you know
you should then that's that's true almost 100% of the time but at the same time if you were to say
well then if you can see it in the chart show me the 10 stocks in the market that are the most
overextended right now how you're gonna see that in the chart you've got to create some filter for
that so you can use something like the RSI or some input to get a list so I think that's one of my
favorite ways to use them and in this case we're looking at outlier situations so I like to use
indicators to spot outlier scenarios some people like to trade off indicators with stocks doing
normal stock things and that's fine I like to set my indicators so that they're very extreme and
we're gonna do this with the RSI in a second because this is this is how we use it but I like to
have my settings in such a way that it's going to alert me to something that is very rare
and in that case it's more meaningful to me when I see it and it's not just so much noise like
I'm looking at this thing all the time and there's all these signals and then I have to decide
if the signal's meaningful or not I'm kind of just following the price action looking at the tape
and then I kind of have my indicators and every once in a while it's like whoa okay like this is
this is this is crazy this is worth my attention um it makes a lot of sense do you ever use them for
anything like just what's normal price action per se or um I would say the my favorite thing to use
that is just a normal cycle is just that volatility cycle that we use which is
anyone who has used or heard of the TTM squeeze it's basically looking at when a stock is super
compressed and that happens all the time stocks get compressed but it's a great way to understand
the stage that the stock isn't and how much energy it has but I'm always combining that with the
price action and the news and whether it's a leader and you know whether the the chart looks good
right so it's not standing on its own
